1: Psalm 130, known since the Latin Vulgate as the De Profundis ("Out of the depths"), is one of the seven traditional penitential psalms (Pss 6, 32, 38, 51, 102, 130, 143), used in Christian liturgy since the early church for Ash Wednesday and the Office of the Dead. It is also the foundation of Luther's most famous chorale, "Aus tiefer Not schrei ich zu dir" (1524), which became a defining text of Reformation theology of grace. "From the depths of despair (mima'amakim) I cry out to you, O LORD" — the mamakim (depths, deeps) are the place of maximum distance from God and from light: the depths of the sea that cover the drowned (Exodus 15:5; Nehemiah 9:11), the depths of Sheol (Psalms 86:13; Isaiah 7:11; Ezekiel 26:20), the depths of distress and alienation. The De Profundis prayer begins at the lowest possible point and goes up.
2: "O Lord, hear my voice; let your ears be open to my pleading (tihenah oznecha qashuvot leqol tahnunai)" — the double petition for auditory attention: "hear my voice" (basic reception) and "let your ears be open to my pleading" (attentive, responsive engagement). Tahanun (pleading, supplication, the prayer that seeks grace) — the root hanan (to be gracious, to show favor) is embedded in the word: the supplication is itself a plea for grace. The address shifts from the tetragrammaton (YHWH) in v1 to Adonai (Lord, my master) — a shift in register from the covenant name to the relational title, emphasizing the petition's personal, dependent character. The ears being "open" (qashuvot, attentive, alert) is the anthropomorphic image of God leaning in to hear, the posture of engaged listening.
3: "If you, O LORD, kept a record of sins (tishmor-avonot), Lord, who could stand before you (mi ya'amod)?" — the foundational argument for divine forgiveness: if YHWH maintained the full, accurate covenant ledger of infractions, no human being could survive divine scrutiny. Tishmor (to keep, guard, watch, preserve — the same word as Ps 121's "keeper"): if YHWH "kept" sins the way a good keeper guards what is entrusted to him, the record would be complete and damning. "Who could stand?" — the legal standing question: the courtroom in which no human being could stand without being condemned immediately. The argument from impossible human righteousness to the necessity of divine forgiveness is the OT's most direct anticipation of Paul's argument in Romans 3: "all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God" (Romans 3:23) requires the divine solution of justification through Christ (Romans 3:24).
4: "But forgiveness is found with you (ki-immekha hasselikhah) — and that is why you are held in awe (lemaan tivvare)" — one of the most paradoxical statements in the Psalter: it is divine forgiveness (not divine wrath) that produces reverential awe. The logic: if YHWH simply maintained the strict record of sins and punished accordingly, the response would be dread-fear, not reverent awe — and humans would flee from the divine presence rather than seek it. It is precisely because YHWH forgives that the covenant community dares to approach, and approaching, discovers the awe of the divine holiness. Selichah (forgiveness, pardon, the release of the debt) appears only three times in the OT (here; Daniel 9:9; Nehemiah 9:17) — always in combination with divine character declarations. The rarity makes its occurrence here emphatic: this is the specific divine attribute being claimed as the basis for prayer.
5: "I wait for the LORD (qivviti YHWH) — my whole being waits — and in his word I put my hope (lidevaro hohhalti)" — the posture of waiting (qavah, to wait with expectation, to hope, to be stretched taut like a bow-string toward the anticipated arrival) is not passive resignation but active, oriented anticipation. "My whole being waits" — the nefesh (inner person, the totality of self) engaged in the waiting: the entire person is oriented toward YHWH's response. "In his word (devaro)" — the ground of the hope is not general optimism but the specific divine promise: the "word" that YHWH has spoken and that has not yet been fulfilled in the psalmist's experience. The hope is anchored in the covenant word, not in the psalmist's own assessment of the situation.
6: "My soul waits for the Lord more than sentinels long for the morning light (shomrim laboker) — yes, more than sentinels wait for morning" — the night watchman on the city wall, standing guard through the darkness and counting the hours until the relief of dawn, is the image of sustained, alert, expectant hope. The sentinel does not sleep — he waits actively, scanning the horizon for the first light of dawn. The repetition "more than sentinels wait for morning... more than sentinels wait for morning" (the phrase occurs twice) functions as staircase parallelism: the second occurrence intensifies and confirms the first. The dawn that the sentinel awaits is inevitable — it will come regardless of how long the night has been. The psalmist's hope in YHWH's response is described with that same inevitability: the morning will come.
7: "O Israel, put your hope in the LORD (yahhel Yisra'el el-YHWH), for with the LORD is steadfast love (hesed), and with him is abundant redemption (harbeh immo fedut)" — the movement from individual petition (Psalms 130:1-6) to corporate exhortation (Psalms 130:7-8): the individual's experience of the depths and the waiting becomes the basis for calling the whole community to the same hope. "With the LORD is hesed" — covenant loyalty, steadfast love, the committed faithfulness of the covenanting God. "Harbeh redemption" (abundant, much, plentiful redemption) — not a scant or barely sufficient redemption but a redemption that exceeds the measure of what needs redeeming. Pedut (redemption, the deliverance that pays the full price) — the ransom-type word that the NT will take up in the theology of Christ as the one who "gave himself as a ransom for many" (Mark 10:45).
8: "He will redeem Israel from all its sins (yifdeh et-Yisra'el mikkol avonotav)" — the psalm closes with the most comprehensive statement: the redemption YHWH offers is total ("from all its sins") and corporate (the entire people). The individual De Profundis prayer, which began in the most personal, isolated experience of depth (v1: "I cry out"), ends with a universal covenant promise covering all of Israel's sins. The individual is carried into the corporate promise; the corporate redemption is experienced by the individual in prayer. The NT will read this verse through the lens of Christ's atoning work: 1 John 1:7 ("the blood of Jesus, his Son, purifies us from all sin") and Romans 11:26 ("all Israel will be saved") both expand the scope of Psalms 130:8's promise through the mechanism of the cross.
